source_metadata.title_raw: Steven J. Block - I-18-250 - February 12, 2019 - Belvedere

content.full_text:
Steven J. Block 
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
February 12, 2019 
213 Golden Gate A venue 
Belvedere, CA 94930 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-18-250 
Dear Mr. Block: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the Act). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict 
of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest laws such as Section 
1090. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 
71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Because you seek 
general information and have not provided information regarding any specific governmental 
decision, we are providing informal assistance. 2 
QUESTIONS 
You are a member of the Board of Trustees of the Marin County Employee Retirement 
Association (MCERA). Your questions relate to whether a conflict of interest will arise because of 
your potential sale of equity securities in a bank (the bank) on the open market or to companies in 
which MCERA investment managers have investments. 
1. Whether your potential sale of bank shares in the open market violates the Act's conflict of 
interest laws because you are an appointed member of the MCERA Board of Trustees. 
2. Whether your sale of bank shares to Barclays Capital (Barclays) and Keene, Bruyette and 
Woods (KB W), pursuant to the underwriting, would violate the Act's conflict of interest 
laws because of your position with MCERA. 
3. Whether any sale of your bank shares to Barclays or KBW would disqualify you from 
taking part in any potential future board decision regarding Barclays, KBW, or other 
affiliated party. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. I-18-250 
Page No. 2 
1. No. Your sale of bank shares as a MCERA board member does not involve a governmental 
decision, and thus is not a conflict of interest under the Act. (Regulation 18704(a)-(b ).) 
2. No. Your sale of bank shares as a MCERA board member does not involve a governmental 
decision, and thus is not a conflict of interest under the Act. (Regulation 18704(a)-(b).) 
3. If you sell shares to Barclays or KBW, you will have an interest in respective business as a 
source of income. In addition, you may have an interest in Stifel Financial Services if you 
sell the stock to KBW and Stifel Financial Services is a parent, subsidiary, or otherwise 
related business of KBW. The Act would prohibit you from taking part in decisions if 
Barclays; KBW; or a parent, subsidiary, or related business entity is explicitly involved in 
the decision. For other decisions where Barclays; KBW; or a subsidiary, parent or related 
business entity is not explicitly involved, you would be prohibited from taking part in the 
decisions only if a prudent person would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
would contribute to a change in the respective business's value or the value of its stock. 
FACTS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You currently hold less than one percent of all the bank's currently outstanding common 
shares. You have held these shares for investment for more than 15 years. You have been informed 
by the bank's management that it is considering an initial public offering of its common stock 
pursuant to a registration statement filed under the Securities Act of 1933. You are informed that 
the bank is negotiating for the underwriting of this offering with Barclays and KBW, and that the 
underwriting will likely include an offer by the underwriters to purchase newly issued shares, 
together with certain shares currently held by the bank's current investors. 
At the request of the bank's management, you have completed a selling stockholder 
questionnaire intended to gauge your interest, and your ability, to sell into the offering. The 
questionnaire does not constitute an offer to buy or sell the bank's securities, but rather to provide 
information to the bank's management for purposes of planning the offering. You have entered no 
agreement regarding the disposition of your bank shares. 
MCERA's Retirement Administrator has informed you that MCERA has no direct business 
with KBW. However, two MCERA investment managers have equity investments with Stifel 
Financial Services, a KBW affiliate. Both of those portfolios are being liquidated. MCERA also has 
no direct relationship with Barclays. However, one ofMCERA's investment managers has equity 
investments with Barclays international branches, and two of MCERA's investment managers hold 
bonds with Barclays or their affiliates. 
ANALYSIS 
Sale of Bank Shares on the Open Market. 
Regulation 18704(a) provides: "A public official makes a governmental decision if the 
official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her 


File No. I-18-250 
Page No. 3 
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her 
agency." Regulation 18704(b) states: "A public official participates in a governmental decision if 
the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the 
decision without significant intervening substantive review." 
Under the foregoing regulatory provisions, your sale of bank shares on the open market 
would not constitute making or participating in making a governmental decision under the Act. 
Consequently, your sale of bank shares would not constitute a disqualifying conflict of interest 
under the Act. (See Regulation 18702.) Moreover, provided your sale of bank shares is on the open 
market and you do not know the identity of the buyer, you would not have a potentially 
disqualifying interest in decisions implicating the unknown buyer. (See Holman.Advice Letter, No. 
A-88-107.) 
Sale of Bank Shares to Barclays or KBW. 
Nothing in the Act precludes you from selling your bank shares to Barclays or KBW while 
serving as a Board member for MCERA because, like selling shares on the open market, you would 
not be making or participating in making a governmental decision under the Act. 
However, Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in 
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the official's interests. (Section 87103.) Section 87103 lists the types of 
financial interests that can give rise to a conflict of interest. Based on the facts provided, the 
following interest applies here: 
• 
Source of Income: A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, 
including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the 
decision (Section 87103(c)). 
Here, should you decide to sell your bank shares, you would have a source of income 
interest in Barclays or KBW, or any purchaser of the shares from whom you receive $500 or more. 
In addition, you may have an interest in any parent, subsidiary, or other related business of Barclays 
or KBW as define~ in Regulation 18700.2(b ), which provides: 
(b) Parent, Subsidiary, Otherwise Related Business Entity, defined. 
(1) Parent - A business entity is a "parent" if it is a corporation that controls 
more than 50 percent of the voting stock of another corporation. The parent 
corporation is also a parent to any subsidiaries of the corporation that it 
controls. 
(2) Subsidiary - A business entity is a "subsidiary" if it is a corporation 
whose voting stock is more than 50 percent controlled by another 
corporation. The subsidiary corporation is also a subsidiary to any 
corporation that controls its parent corporation. 


FileNo. 1-18-250 
Page No. 4 
(3) Otherwise related business entity. Business entities, other than a parent 
corporation as defined in subdivision (b )(1 ), are otherwise related if: 
(A) The same person or persons together direct or control each business 
entity; or 
(B) The same person or persons together have a 50 percent or greater ownership 
interest in each business entity. 
Foreseeability and Materiality. 
Foreseeability standards vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a 
governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is 
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 1870l(a).) Regulation 1870l(a) 
establishes that a financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real 
property financial interest as described in Regulation l 8702.2(a)(l )-(6). 
For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial 
effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 1870l(b).) 
MCERA Decisions Regarding Barclays, KBW, or Stifel Financial Services. 
You have inquired about MCERA decisions that involve Barclays or KBW. In situations 
where Barclays or KBW are explicitly involved in an MCERA decision within 12 months of the 
sale of your shares, the financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable (Regulation 1870l(a)), 
and the financial effect will be material if a provision under Regulation 18702.1 (a)(l) through (a)(7) 
applies. Moreover, if the business entity is not explicitly involved in a MCERA decision, then the 
"financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price 
of the business entity's publicly traded stock or the value of a privately-held business entity." 
(Regulation 18702.1 (b).) 
At this time, you have not identified any particular decision. Accordingly, we can only 
generally advise that if you sell the shares to Barclays or KBW, you are potentially disqualified 
from any decision in which the respective business is explicitly involved as well as any decision in 
which the respective business is not explicitly involved if the decision may affect the value of the 
business or the value of the business's stock. 
In addition, you have identified Stifel Financial Services as an "affiliate" of KBW. To the 
extent that Stifel is a parent, subsidiary, or otherwise related business of KBW and you sell your 
stock to KBW, you are likewise potentially disqualified from any decision in which the Stifel is 


File No. I-18-250 
Page No. 5 
explicitly involved, as well as any decision in which the business is not explicitly involved if the 
decision may affect Stifel's value or the value of its stock. 3 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
/ 
By: 
JMF:jgl 
Sincerely, 
ni 
Commission Counsel 
Legal Division 
3 Note that the determination of a conflict of interest is dependent upon the facts involved in each decision. 
Therefore, we recommend you contact our office for additional advice with respect to particular decisions before 
MCERA.

QUESTION: You are a member of the Board of Trustees of the Marin County Employee Retirement 
Association (MCERA). Your questions relate to whether a conflict of interest will arise because of 
your potential sale of equity securities in a bank (the bank) on the open market or to companies in 
which MCERA investment managers have investments.

2. Whether your sale of bank shares to Barclays Capital (Barclays) and Keene, Bruyette and 
Woods (KB W), pursuant to the underwriting, would violate the Act's conflict of interest 
laws because of your position with MCERA. 
3. Whether any sale of your bank shares to Barclays or KBW would disqualify you from 
taking part in any potential future board decision regarding Barclays, KBW, or other 
affiliated party.2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).)

CONCLUSION: 1. No. Your sale of bank shares as a MCERA board member does not involve a governmental 
decision, and thus is not a conflict of interest under the Act. (Regulation 18704(a)-(b ).) 
2. No. Your sale of bank shares as a MCERA board member does not involve a governmental 
decision, and thus is not a conflict of interest under the Act. (Regulation 18704(a)-(b).) 
3. If you sell shares to Barclays or KBW, you will have an interest in respective business as a 
source of income. In addition, you may have an interest in Stifel Financial Services if you 
sell the stock to KBW and Stifel Financial Services is a parent, subsidiary, or otherwise 
related business of KBW. The Act would prohibit you from taking part in decisions if
